skill.name: pysam
skill.description: Guides pysam workflows for reading, writing, querying, indexing, and summarizing genomic SAM/BAM/CRAM, VCF/BCF, FASTA, and FASTQ files.
skill.summary: Use pysam to inspect, filter, index, and summarize common genomic sequencing file formats.
skill.author: davila7

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many critical and high patterns, but semantic review found most are false positives from bioinformatics terminology and pysam APIs. SAM refers to Sequence Alignment/Map files, fetch() is local genomic region retrieval, and weak cryptography hits map to nucleotide terms or documentation text. The remaining risk is medium because the skill teaches local file writes, index creation, and samtools/bcftools wrapper calls that can modify files when used with untrusted paths.

medium_findings.title: Wrapped samtools and bcftools Operations
medium_findings.description: The skill documents pysam.samtools and pysam.bcftools wrapper calls for sorting, indexing, viewing, and compressing genomic files. This is legitimate for pysam, but it can modify local files and should use trusted filenames and explicit user approval for destructive outputs.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command-wrapper examples are direct and semantically confirmed, but they are expected pysam functionality rather than evidence of malicious intent.

medium_findings.title: Local Genomic File Writes
medium_findings.description: The examples write BAM, VCF, FASTQ, FASTA, CSV, and bedGraph outputs. This is normal for data processing, but careless generated paths can overwrite files or create sensitive derived genomic datasets.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: Multiple file-writing examples are present and easy to verify. The risk depends on user-provided paths and data sensitivity, so confidence is high for capability but moderate for impact.

low_findings.title: SAM Credential Findings Are False Positives
low_findings.description: Static critical hits for the Windows SAM database are false positives. In this skill, SAM means Sequence Alignment/Map, a standard genomic alignment file format.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The surrounding text explicitly defines SAM/BAM/CRAM alignment files for genomic data. No evidence found of Windows credential database access.

low_findings.title: Network Fetch Findings Are False Positives
low_findings.description: Static network detections for fetch() are false positives. The calls use pysam file APIs to retrieve genomic regions from local indexed data, not HTTP or external network access.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code uses samfile.fetch, vcf.fetch, and fasta.fetch on local file objects. No evidence found of Fetch API, sockets, URLs, or data exfiltration.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography and Scanner Tool Hits Are Lexical False Positives
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography and network scanning alerts do not match dangerous behavior in context. The reviewed lines describe genomic quality fields, nucleotide ambiguity codes, and mapping terms rather than cryptographic algorithms or scanner execution.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The contextual terms are domain-specific genomics language. No evidence found of MD5/SHA1 hashing use, nmap execution, or network reconnaissance intent.

dangerous_patterns.title: Command-Style Native Tool Wrappers
dangerous_patterns.description: pysam exposes samtools and bcftools wrappers that behave like command-line operations and can create, sort, index, or compress files. Treat generated arguments as file-system operations that require trusted inputs.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The wrapper calls are documented directly. They are not malicious by themselves, but they are the main confirmed operational risk.

dangerous_patterns.title: Output File Creation and Overwrite Risk
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill includes examples that create output files and indexes, including a force=True tabix indexing example. Generated code should avoid overwriting existing files without explicit confirmation.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The file-creation behavior is explicit and repeated. Impact depends on user paths, but overwrite risk is a realistic concern for generated workflows.

content.value_statement: Genomic workflows require careful handling of BAM, CRAM, VCF, BCF, FASTA, and FASTQ files. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code through pysam patterns for reading, filtering, indexing, and summarizing those files.
actual_capabilities: Explains how to open, read, and write SAM, BAM, and CRAM alignment files with pysam.AlignmentFile.
Shows how to inspect VCF and BCF records, headers, genotypes, INFO fields, and sample data.
Guides FASTA and FASTQ sequence access, quality filtering, sequence extraction, and format conversion.
Documents region-based queries, coordinate system differences, pileup analysis, and coverage workflows.
Provides examples for creating indexes with pysam, samtools, bcftools, and tabix wrappers.
Combines alignment, variant, and sequence files in practical bioinformatics workflows.
limitations: It is documentation and prompt guidance, not a replacement for the pysam package or htslib tools.
Examples assume users provide valid genomic files, indexes, headers, and coordinate systems.
Generated workflows may need adjustment for large cohorts, unusual reference genomes, or custom annotations.
The skill does not validate privacy, consent, or compliance requirements for human genomic data.

use_cases.title: Build sequencing quality checks
use_cases.description: Create scripts that count reads, mapping rates, duplicates, coverage, and low-quality regions in BAM or CRAM files.

use_cases.title: Inspect and filter variants
use_cases.description: Work with VCF or BCF records, sample genotypes, INFO annotations, and region filters for variant analysis.

use_cases.title: Extract reference and read sequences
use_cases.description: Retrieve FASTA regions, process FASTQ reads, convert formats, and prepare sequence subsets for downstream tools.

prompt_templates.title: Open a genomic file
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner orientation for a single file type
prompt_templates.prompt: Show me how to open a [BAM, VCF, FASTA, or FASTQ] file with pysam and print the most important fields.

prompt_templates.title: Filter reads or variants
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a pysam workflow that filters [reads or variants] from [input file] by [quality, region, or sample] and writes a new output file.

prompt_templates.title: Calculate coverage or summary metrics
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a pysam script that calculates coverage and quality metrics for [regions] in [BAM or CRAM file], with clear output columns.

prompt_templates.title: Integrate multiple genomic files
prompt_templates.prompt: Plan a pysam pipeline that combines [BAM], [VCF], and [FASTA] data to validate variants, annotate coverage, and report edge cases.

output_examples.input: Ask for a BAM quality control workflow for one chromosome.
output_examples.output: A concise plan with read counts, mapping rate, duplicate rate, coverage steps, index requirements, and output file precautions.

output_examples.input: Ask how to inspect genotype fields in a VCF file.
output_examples.output: A field guide explaining headers, samples, genotype tuples, missing values, allele frequency checks, and coordinate conventions.

output_examples.input: Ask for FASTQ filtering guidance based on read quality.
output_examples.output: A workflow description covering sequential reads, mean quality calculation, output naming, compression expectations, and validation checks.

best_practices: Confirm coordinate conventions before mixing pysam numeric ranges, region strings, and VCF positions.
Use indexed files for region queries and document how each index was created.
Write outputs to explicit new paths and confirm before overwriting genomic data or indexes.
anti_patterns: Do not assume SAM means Windows credentials in this context; it is a genomic file format.
Do not run generated samtools or bcftools wrapper calls on untrusted paths without review.
Do not load whole large sequencing files into memory when streaming or indexed access is available.

faq.question: What file formats does this skill cover?
faq.answer: It covers SAM, BAM, CRAM, VCF, BCF, FASTA, FASTQ, and tabix-indexed genomic annotation files.

faq.question: Does this skill install pysam?
faq.answer: No. It provides guidance and examples. Users still need a working Python environment with pysam installed.

faq.question: Why are indexes important?
faq.answer: Indexes allow fast region queries for BAM, CRAM, FASTA, compressed VCF, BCF, and tabix-compatible files.

faq.question: Can this skill work with human genomic data?
faq.answer: Yes, but users must handle privacy, consent, access control, and local compliance requirements outside the skill.

faq.question: Are the samtools and bcftools examples safe?
faq.answer: They are normal pysam usage, but they can modify files. Review paths and outputs before running generated commands.

faq.question: What is the most common source of errors?
faq.answer: Coordinate confusion is common. Numeric pysam ranges are often 0-based, while VCF positions and region strings may be 1-based.
